news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking advances in quantum memory, innovative imaging techniques, enhanced digital identity security, and unprecedented support for emerging researchers through record NSF PhD fellowships.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires dans le domaine de la mémoire quantique, des techniques d'imagerie innovantes, une sécurité renforcée de l'identité numérique, ainsi qu'un soutien sans précédent aux jeunes chercheurs grâce à un nombre record de bourses de doctorat accordées par la NSF.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en memoria cuántica, técnicas de imagen innovadoras, una mayor seguridad de la identidad digital y un apoyo sin precedentes a los investigadores noveles a través de un número récord de becas de doctorado de la NSF.

articles.title: NSF awards record number of coveted PhD fellowships in surprise move
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 14 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01119-1Quantum science and AI research are big winners just a year after the US funding giant slashed its Graduate Research Fellowship Program awards in half.
articles.content:
Graduate students in the field of quantum computing are among those to receive Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP) awards this year.Credit: gorodenkoff/Getty

In an unexpected turnaround, the US National Science Foundation (NSF) on Sunday handed out a record 2,599 of its prestigious graduate fellowships to young researchers — after briefly slashing the number to a low of just 1,000 last year.

The rebound “is a significant boost for early-career researchers and the future of US science”, says Joshua Weitz, a biologist at the University of Maryland in College Park.

Massive budget cuts for US science proposed again by Trump administration

The surprise increase comes at a time when many in the US science community have been worrying about the fate of the NSF, a major funder of basic science, and its Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP). Last year, and again this year, the administration of US President Donald Trump called to cut the NSF’s budget by more than half.

The GRFP seemed to be in further trouble when the call for the programme’s 2026 applications went out more than two months late, and the eligibility criteria for the fellowships changed. As of this year, undergraduates and first-year master’s and PhD students can apply — but second-year graduate students, as had been the norm for decades, cannot. Between January and April, at least 65 applications were also ‘returned without review’ (sent back to applicants without a score), according to data shared with Nature by Grant Witness, a watchdog project that tracks changes to research funding. This has sparked concerns that the type of science that NSF would fund had also changed.

But some of these worries have been allayed with the announcement of a record-breaking number of GRFP awardees this year.

Brian Stone, who is standing in as NSF director until a permanent one is confirmed, said in a statement that the continuation of the programme reflects the Trump administration’s “strong focus on building talent and investing in individual researchers”. He added: “I’m excited to see how these emerging STEM [science, technology, engineering and mathematics] leaders will shape the future.”

The agency did not respond to Nature’s queries about changes to the programme or applications returned without review. On its website, the NSF states that “the number of applications returned without review this year has not changed substantially since last year”.

Award winners

Almost 14,000 young researchers applied for a 2026 GRFP award, submitting a research plan and personal statement that were reviewed by an independent panel of researchers. Typically, only about one in every six applicants receive one of the prestigious fellowships.

NSF slashes prestigious PhD fellowship awards by half

In addition to covering tuition, the fellowships come with an annual stipend of US$37,000 for three years. Since 1952, when the GRFP began, it has supported more than 70,000 researchers, and at least 40 of those have gone on to receive Nobel prizes.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant positive development in US science funding with the NSF awarding a record number of prestigious graduate fellowships, supporting early-career researchers in critical STEM fields like quantum computing and AI. This has broad implications for the future of science and technology innovation in the US, benefiting a large community of researchers and society at large.
articles.personality_title: NSF awards record number of graduate fellowships to support young scientists
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The US National Science Foundation (NSF) supports young researchers through its Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP). This program provides funding to graduate students in science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) fields. Last year, the NSF cut the number of fellowships in half, causing concern among scientists.

**What happened** – In 2026, the NSF surprised many by awarding a record 2,599 fellowships, more than doubling last year’s number. Nearly 14,000 students applied, with winners receiving a stipend of $37,000 per year for three years plus tuition support. This year, new rules allowed undergraduates and first-year graduate students to apply, while second-year students were not eligible.

**Impact** – This large increase helps many early-career researchers, especially in important areas like quantum computing and artificial intelligence. These fellowships not only provide money but also recognition that can boost careers. Since 1952, the program has supported over 70,000 researchers, including several Nobel Prize winners.

**What's next step** – The NSF will continue to fund these fellowships and support STEM research. The program’s changes and increased awards may encourage more students to enter important scientific fields. Future applicants will watch closely to see if this positive trend continues.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The NSF’s record number of fellowships in 2026 offers strong support to thousands of young scientists, helping to advance key fields like quantum computing and AI in the US.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NSF attribue un nombre record de bourses d’études aux jeunes chercheurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La National Science Foundation (NSF) des États-Unis soutient les jeunes chercheurs grâce à son programme de bourses de recherche pour les diplômés (GRFP). Ce programme finance les étudiants en sciences, technologie, ingénierie et mathématiques (STEM). L’année dernière, la NSF avait réduit de moitié le nombre de bourses, inquiétant la communauté scientifique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, la NSF a surpris en attribuant un nombre record de 2 599 bourses, soit plus du double de l’année précédente. Près de 14 000 étudiants ont postulé, et les lauréats reçoivent une allocation annuelle de 37 000 dollars pendant trois ans, en plus de la prise en charge des frais de scolarité. Cette année, les étudiants de premier cycle et de première année de master ou doctorat pouvaient postuler, alors que les étudiants de deuxième année n’étaient pas éligibles.

**Impact** – Cette forte augmentation aide de nombreux jeunes chercheurs, notamment dans des domaines importants comme l’informatique quantique et l’intelligence artificielle. Ces bourses apportent non seulement un soutien financier, mais aussi une reconnaissance qui peut booster les carrières. Depuis 1952, le programme a soutenu plus de 70 000 chercheurs, dont plusieurs lauréats du prix Nobel.

**Prochaine étape** – La NSF continuera à financer ces bourses et à soutenir la recherche STEM. Les changements et l’augmentation des bourses pourraient encourager davantage d’étudiants à se tourner vers ces domaines scientifiques clés. Les futurs candidats suivront de près cette tendance positive.

**Phrase clé** – Le nombre record de bourses attribuées par la NSF en 2026 offre un fort soutien à des milliers de jeunes scientifiques, aidant à faire avancer des domaines clés comme l’informatique quantique et l’IA aux États-Unis.
articles.personality_title_es: La NSF otorga un número récord de becas a jóvenes investigadores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Fundación Nacional de Ciencias (NSF) de Estados Unidos apoya a jóvenes investigadores con su Programa de Becas para Graduados (GRFP). Este programa financia a estudiantes de posgrado en ciencias, tecnología, ingeniería y matemáticas (STEM). El año pasado, la NSF redujo a la mitad el número de becas, lo que preocupó a la comunidad científica.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, la NSF sorprendió a muchos al otorgar un récord de 2,599 becas, más del doble que el año anterior. Casi 14,000 estudiantes aplicaron, y los ganadores reciben una beca anual de 37,000 dólares durante tres años, además de cubrir la matrícula. Este año, estudiantes de pregrado y primer año de posgrado pudieron aplicar, mientras que los de segundo año no fueron elegibles.

**Impacto** – Este gran aumento ayuda a muchos jóvenes investigadores, especialmente en áreas importantes como la computación cuántica y la inteligencia artificial. Estas becas no solo ofrecen apoyo económico, sino también reconocimiento que puede impulsar sus carreras. Desde 1952, el programa ha apoyado a más de 70,000 investigadores, incluidos varios ganadores del Premio Nobel.

**Próximo paso** – La NSF seguirá financiando estas becas y apoyando la investigación STEM. Los cambios y el aumento en las becas pueden motivar a más estudiantes a ingresar a campos científicos clave. Los futuros solicitantes estarán atentos para ver si esta tendencia positiva continúa.

**Frase clave** – El número récord de becas otorgadas por la NSF en 2026 brinda un fuerte apoyo a miles de jóvenes científicos, ayudando a avanzar en campos clave como la computación cuántica y la IA en Estados Unidos.

articles.title: Quantum systems can remember and forget at the same time, scientists discover
articles.summary: Quantum systems can secretly “remember” their past—even when they appear not to. Scientists found that whether a system shows memory depends on how you look at it: through its evolving state or its measurable properties. Each perspective uncovers different kinds of memory, meaning a system can seem memoryless and memory-filled at the same time. This discovery could change how researchers design and control quantum technologies.
articles.content:
An international team of scientists has taken a closer look at how memory works in quantum systems and uncovered a surprising result. Their research shows that a quantum process can seem completely memoryless when viewed one way, yet still retain memory when examined from another angle. This unexpected finding opens the door to new lines of research in quantum science and technology.

In classical physics, the idea of memory is straightforward. A system is considered memoryless if its future behavior depends only on its current state. If past states continue to influence what happens next, the system is said to have memory.

Quantum physics is far less clear-cut. Quantum systems can store and transfer information in ways that have no classical counterpart, and measurements themselves play a key role in how these systems evolve. Because of this, defining memory in quantum mechanics has remained a challenge.

Rethinking Memory in Quantum Systems

In a study published in PRX Quantum, researchers from the University of Turku in Finland, the University of Milan in Italy, and Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń in Poland revisited the concept of memory in quantum systems to better understand its meaning.

"Our work shows that memory is not a single concept but can manifest in different ways depending on how the evolution of a system is described," says first author, Doctoral Researcher Federico Settimo from the University of Turku.

Two Perspectives in Quantum Mechanics

Scientists have long studied memory by tracking how quantum states change over time, an approach rooted in the work of Erwin Schrödinger. However, quantum theory also offers another equally important framework developed by Werner Heisenberg. Instead of focusing on states, this perspective looks at how observable quantities evolve, meaning the measurable properties seen in experiments.

Although both approaches produce the same experimental results, the new research shows they are not interchangeable when it comes to describing memory.

Hidden Memory Effects Revealed

The team found that these two perspectives can reveal different types of memory. Some memory effects only appear when analyzing the evolution of quantum states, while others become visible only when focusing on observables.

This means a quantum system can appear memoryless in one description but show clear signs of memory in another. The finding suggests that quantum memory is more complex than previously believed and cannot be fully understood by looking at quantum states alone.

Implications for Quantum Technology

"Our findings open up new research avenues into the dynamics of quantum systems. Moreover, our work has implications beyond its foundational significance for quantum technologies, where the external environment induces noise and memory effects. Knowing how memory can be witnessed is essential for developing strategies to mitigate noise or exploit environmental effects in realistic quantum devices," says Professor of Theoretical Physics Jyrki Piilo from the University of Turku.

By clarifying how memory works in quantum systems, the study sheds new light on a fundamental aspect of quantum dynamics. It also highlights how the unique nature of quantum time evolution reshapes even basic concepts like memory, with potential consequences for future technologies.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about quantum memory that clarifies fundamental quantum dynamics and opens new research avenues. This breakthrough has broad implications for the development and control of quantum technologies, which could benefit society by advancing computing, communication, and other quantum applications. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful advancement with potential large-scale impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover quantum systems can both remember and forget at once
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how memory works in quantum systems, where particles can behave in strange ways unlike everyday objects. Understanding memory in these systems is important for developing new quantum technologies like super-fast computers.

**What happened** – Researchers from Finland, Italy, and Poland found that quantum systems can show memory or no memory depending on how you look at them. By studying two different ways to describe quantum systems—one focusing on their changing states, the other on measurable properties—they discovered a system can seem to remember its past or appear forgetful at the same time.

**Impact** – This finding changes how scientists understand memory in quantum physics. It shows that memory is not a simple idea but depends on the viewpoint used. This deeper knowledge helps researchers design better quantum devices, since memory effects can affect how these devices work and how noise from the environment influences them.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore these different memory types more closely to develop new methods for controlling quantum systems. This could lead to improved quantum technologies that are more reliable and efficient in computing, communication, and sensing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Quantum systems can both remember and forget simultaneously, revealing a new complexity in quantum memory that could improve future technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent que les systèmes quantiques peuvent à la fois se souvenir et oublier
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps comment fonctionne la mémoire dans les systèmes quantiques, où les particules agissent de manière étrange, différente des objets quotidiens. Comprendre la mémoire dans ces systèmes est important pour développer de nouvelles technologies quantiques comme les ordinateurs ultra-rapides.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de Finlande, d'Italie et de Pologne ont découvert que les systèmes quantiques peuvent montrer de la mémoire ou ne pas en montrer selon la façon dont on les observe. En étudiant deux manières différentes de décrire ces systèmes — une basée sur leurs états changeants, l'autre sur leurs propriétés mesurables — ils ont trouvé qu’un système peut paraître se souvenir de son passé ou oublier en même temps.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon dont les scientifiques comprennent la mémoire en physique quantique. Elle montre que la mémoire n’est pas une idée simple, mais dépend du point de vue utilisé. Cette connaissance approfondie aide à concevoir de meilleurs dispositifs quantiques, car les effets de mémoire influencent leur fonctionnement et la façon dont le bruit extérieur agit sur eux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier plus en détail ces différents types de mémoire pour développer de nouvelles méthodes de contrôle des systèmes quantiques. Cela pourrait améliorer les technologies quantiques en les rendant plus fiables et efficaces dans le calcul, la communication et la détection.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les systèmes quantiques peuvent à la fois se souvenir et oublier, révélant une nouvelle complexité de la mémoire quantique qui pourrait améliorer les technologies futures.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que los sistemas cuánticos pueden recordar y olvidar al mismo tiempo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo funciona la memoria en los sistemas cuánticos, donde las partículas se comportan de formas extrañas diferentes a los objetos cotidianos. Entender la memoria en estos sistemas es clave para desarrollar nuevas tecnologías cuánticas como computadoras súper rápidas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Finlandia, Italia y Polonia encontraron que los sistemas cuánticos pueden mostrar memoria o no, dependiendo de cómo se los observe. Al estudiar dos formas diferentes de describir estos sistemas — una enfocada en sus estados cambiantes y otra en sus propiedades medibles — descubrieron que un sistema puede parecer recordar su pasado o parecer olvidadizo al mismo tiempo.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia la manera en que los científicos entienden la memoria en la física cuántica. Muestra que la memoria no es una idea simple, sino que depende del punto de vista usado. Este conocimiento más profundo ayuda a diseñar mejores dispositivos cuánticos, ya que los efectos de memoria afectan cómo funcionan y cómo el ruido ambiental los influye.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán más estos diferentes tipos de memoria para crear nuevos métodos para controlar los sistemas cuánticos. Esto podría llevar a tecnologías cuánticas más confiables y eficientes en computación, comunicación y detección.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los sistemas cuánticos pueden recordar y olvidar al mismo tiempo, revelando una nueva complejidad en la memoria cuántica que podría mejorar las tecnologías futuras.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned abstract painting depicting two intertwined translucent spirals—one representing quantum states and the other observables—each revealing different glowing patterns within, symbolizing simultaneous memory and forgetfulness in a quantum system, set against a soft gradient background of natural blues and gentle earth tones.

articles.title: Imaging interface-controlled bulk oxygen spillover
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10324-xIn situ microscopic single-particle imaging demonstrates the significance of rationally engineered metal–support interfaces for activating the oxygen in bulk catalyst, helping elucidate reaction pathways in catalytic conversions.
articles.content: As one dynamic aspect of catalysis, spillover is known as species diffusion between an active metal and its support1,2,3, especially in reactions involving hydrogen and oxygen4,5,6,7,8. Spillover confined on the catalyst surface has been investigated extensively9,10; however, it remains unclear whether the bulk catalyst participates in the reactions through non-surface spillover. Here we track the oxygen spillover in Ru/TiO 2 catalysts using in situ environmental transmission electron microscopy. Lattice oxygen was found to transport directly from the TiO 2 substrate to the supported Ru particles through the Ru/TiO 2 interface instead of the traditionally expected surface diffusion11. As a result, the TiO 2 lattice at the subsurface was strained reversibly to provide channels for oxygen transport, as detected by the picometre-precision tracing of atomic displacement. The structural adaptability at the metal–support interface is critical for controlling oxygen spillover, which is switched on in Ru/rutile-TiO 2 but switched off in Ru/anatase-TiO 2 . As shown by the real-time atom-resolved evidence, this bulk oxygen spillover is generally viable in supported metal catalysts of an interfacial epitaxy nature and demonstrates the significance of rationally engineered metal–support interfaces for activating the oxygen in bulk catalyst to contribute to reactions.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding oxygen spillover mechanisms in catalysts, which has broad implications for improving catalytic reactions. This advancement in catalyst design can lead to more efficient chemical processes with potential large-scale industrial and environmental benefits. The study provides detailed, substantive evidence and focuses on a clear, impactful discovery in catalyst technology.
articles.personality_title: New insights reveal how oxygen moves inside catalysts to boost chemical reactions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Catalysts are materials that speed up chemical reactions, often by helping oxygen or hydrogen move between tiny parts called metals and their supports. Scientists have studied this movement, called spillover, mostly on the surface of catalysts, but they weren’t sure if oxygen could move inside the bulk of the catalyst.

**What happened** – Researchers used a special microscope that works in real conditions to watch oxygen moving inside a catalyst made of ruthenium (Ru) and titanium dioxide (TiO2). They discovered that oxygen travels directly through the interface between Ru and TiO2 inside the material, not just on the surface as previously thought. This movement causes tiny changes in the TiO2 structure, opening pathways for oxygen to flow.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it changes how scientists understand oxygen’s role in catalysts. It shows that the inside of the catalyst, especially the connection between metal and support, plays a key role in reactions. Knowing this can help design better catalysts that work more efficiently, potentially saving energy and reducing pollution in industries that use these reactions.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely explore how to control these metal-support interfaces to improve catalyst performance for various chemical processes. This could lead to new materials that activate oxygen more effectively and make industrial reactions cleaner and faster.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered that oxygen moves inside catalysts through metal-support interfaces, offering new ways to design more efficient chemical reactions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelles découvertes sur le déplacement de l’oxygène à l’intérieur des catalyseurs pour améliorer les réactions chimiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les catalyseurs sont des matériaux qui accélèrent les réactions chimiques en aidant souvent l’oxygène ou l’hydrogène à se déplacer entre de petites parties appelées métaux et leurs supports. Les scientifiques ont surtout étudié ce déplacement, appelé migration, à la surface des catalyseurs, mais ils ne savaient pas si l’oxygène pouvait se déplacer à l’intérieur du catalyseur.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont utilisé un microscope spécial fonctionnant dans des conditions réelles pour observer l’oxygène se déplaçant à l’intérieur d’un catalyseur composé de ruthénium (Ru) et de dioxyde de titane (TiO2). Ils ont découvert que l’oxygène circule directement à travers l’interface entre Ru et TiO2 à l’intérieur du matériau, et pas seulement à la surface comme on le pensait auparavant. Ce déplacement provoque de petits changements dans la structure du TiO2, ouvrant des voies pour le passage de l’oxygène.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle change la compréhension du rôle de l’oxygène dans les catalyseurs. Elle montre que l’intérieur du catalyseur, en particulier la connexion entre le métal et le support, joue un rôle clé dans les réactions. Cela peut aider à concevoir de meilleurs catalyseurs, plus efficaces, qui économisent de l’énergie et réduisent la pollution dans les industries utilisant ces réactions.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement chercher à contrôler ces interfaces métal-support pour améliorer la performance des catalyseurs dans divers procédés chimiques. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouveaux matériaux activant l’oxygène plus efficacement et rendant les réactions industrielles plus propres et plus rapides.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que l’oxygène se déplace à l’intérieur des catalyseurs via les interfaces métal-support, ouvrant la voie à la conception de réactions chimiques plus efficaces.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos hallazgos muestran cómo se mueve el oxígeno dentro de los catalizadores para mejorar las reacciones químicas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los catalizadores son materiales que aceleran las reacciones químicas ayudando a que el oxígeno o el hidrógeno se muevan entre pequeñas partes llamadas metales y sus soportes. Los científicos han estudiado este movimiento, llamado derrame, principalmente en la superficie de los catalizadores, pero no estaban seguros si el oxígeno podía moverse dentro del catalizador.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores usaron un microscopio especial que funciona en condiciones reales para observar el oxígeno moviéndose dentro de un catalizador hecho de rutenio (Ru) y dióxido de titanio (TiO2). Descubrieron que el oxígeno viaja directamente a través de la interfaz entre Ru y TiO2 dentro del material, no solo en la superficie como se pensaba antes. Este movimiento causa pequeños cambios en la estructura del TiO2, abriendo caminos para que el oxígeno fluya.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque cambia cómo los científicos entienden el papel del oxígeno en los catalizadores. Muestra que el interior del catalizador, especialmente la conexión entre el metal y el soporte, tiene un papel clave en las reacciones. Saber esto puede ayudar a diseñar mejores catalizadores que funcionen con más eficiencia, ahorrando energía y reduciendo la contaminación en industrias que usan estas reacciones.

**Próximo paso** – Probablemente, los científicos explorarán cómo controlar estas interfaces metal-soporte para mejorar el desempeño de los catalizadores en varios procesos químicos. Esto podría llevar a nuevos materiales que activen el oxígeno de manera más efectiva y hagan las reacciones industriales más limpias y rápidas.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos descubrieron que el oxígeno se mueve dentro de los catalizadores a través de las interfaces metal-soporte, ofreciendo nuevas formas de diseñar reacciones químicas más eficientes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of interconnected crystalline structures symbolizing TiO2 and Ru particles, with delicate, glowing oxygen atoms flowing seamlessly through an intricate lattice bridge between them, highlighting reversible strain and dynamic interface channels in soft, natural hues of blue, gray, and amber.

articles.title: Researchers: here’s how to audit your fragmented digital identity
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 14 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-025-04132-yServices that track researcher profiles can splinter people’s online identities — but there are ways to keep them consistent.
articles.content:
Databases that track academic publications can create duplicate researcher profiles, misattribute citations and list incorrect affiliations.Credit: Vasilina Popova / Getty

When news broke last November that Guo Wei, a well-known researcher in China, had been suspended after serious discrepancies were found in his stated qualifications, social-media pundits quickly swooped in to ask the obvious question: how did no one notice earlier?

It is tempting to assume that a quick online search would have exposed inconsistencies in his education, affiliations and publication record. So, shortly after the news broke, we set out to test this assumption, drawing on our experience as librarians working with researchers’ digital identities.

A search for ‘Guo Wei’ in ORCID, a global system for tracking academics and their research activity, returned 616 profiles. None of them had been affiliated with the Jiangsu University of Science and Technology in Zhenjiang, China, the organization that fired the researcher for misconduct. A search of Scopus, a prominent bibliographic database, produced 615 author records, three of which were nominally linked to the university, each listing a single publication. There was no reliable way to tell whether any of these records belonged to the same person. Even a diligent hiring panel would have struggled to verify who they were looking at, illustrating how even basic searches can become complicated when digital identities are fragmented.

Our experiences tell us that these scattered online profiles are becoming more common in science — and not just because of fraudsters. We feel that it’s crucial for academics to keep on top of their online profiles to collate a consistent image of themselves for potential collaborators, review panels and colleagues.

How identity ambiguity begins

Several digital platforms have tried to solve the problem of mismatched academic identities. ORCID, the social-media site LinkedIn, indexing databases and preprint servers all go to some lengths to distinguish between researchers who share the same name. But these platforms update according to their own schedules and have a variety of rules for their users. Even on a single platform, some details are captured more reliably than others. Scopus, for instance, receives information from the journals that it indexes, but the author profiles it produces do not always reflect a complete affiliation history.

A new form of CV for researchers

A researcher who moves institutions or publishes under several name variations (such as ‘Guo Wei’ and ‘Guo W.’) can accumulate several profilexs without realizing it, and people often stop managing old records. Moreover, anyone with a common name might be one of dozens of digital duplicates. These scattered records rarely create serious problems, but they often distort how researchers are seen: papers can drop out of databases, metrics can change unpredictably, affiliations can be unreliable and researchers might not receive full credit for the work that they have done. Long before the Guo case surfaced, we had already seen these problems first hand. In 2023, we examined the digital identities of all of the researchers at our own institution — the University of Bath, UK, where we work as research librarians — to understand the effects of ambiguous online identities. What we found made the scale of the issue clear and shaped the practical steps that we took to inform our colleagues of how to tidy up their online presence.

What our audit uncovered

Between 2023 and 2024, we reviewed more than 1,000 staff profiles across three platforms: ORCID, Pure and Scopus. Pure is the system that our university uses to manage researchers’ affiliations, publications and funding information. Scopus is the indexing database used most widely across the disciplines researched at the University of Bath. We undertook this review to improve the accuracy of our researchers’ digital profiles, which shape how our staff members are regarded externally. Our review was internal and so has not been peer reviewed or published.

We sent out personalized reports to the academics whose profiles we had assessed, comparing how their individual publication data were represented by different platforms. We found empty ORCID records, duplicate ORCID and Scopus profiles, and misattributed and missing publications. These errors often persist because platforms rely on researchers and institutions to flag and correct errors. On the basis of this work, we created short videos for our library website to help researchers to review and tidy up their digital profiles.

How can I break into industry if my CV keeps disappearing into a black hole?

What happened next was the most revealing part of the exercise. Once our colleagues had received their reports, many corrected their profiles within days. At our university, most researchers already had an ORCID identifier, but the number of people who had linked their ORCID profile to their Scopus one rose sharply, resolving many of the mismatches. We also ensured that ORCID and Scopus author identifiers were recorded in Pure for all staff members, which made it easier for the institutional system to match publications to the correct researchers and improve data quality.

One professor discovered that nearly half of his publications and more than half of his citations were sitting in a second Scopus profile that he did not know existed. After the records were merged, his citation count rose from around 5,500 to more than 12,000, and his online presence finally reflected his actual body of work. Others had similar experiences. “I had not realized how much of my work was scattered until I saw it all together,” one researcher said. Another added: “Two profiles I did not even know I had were merged in minutes.” For many, the process provided clarity and a renewed sense of control.

By early 2024, most researchers at the University of Bath had aligned their ORCID profiles with their institutional record and, when relevant, with their Scopus author profiles. Once the gaps were visible, the fixes were straightforward and the benefits immediate.

The experiences we gained from reviewing others’ profiles have shaped the guidance that we offer here.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful initiative to audit and correct fragmented digital identities of researchers, which improves accuracy and credit attribution in academic publishing. This has broad significance for the global research community by enhancing transparency, recognition, and collaboration. The story is focused on a clear problem and solution with detailed examples and practical outcomes, demonstrating a positive real-world impact on the academic ecosystem.
articles.personality_title: University audit helps researchers fix scattered online profiles
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many researchers have multiple online profiles that list their work, but these profiles can be incomplete, duplicated, or incorrect. This makes it hard for others to see all of a researcher’s work or check their background accurately.

**What happened** – At the University of Bath in the UK, librarians reviewed over 1,000 researchers’ profiles across three platforms: ORCID, Pure, and Scopus. They found many errors like empty profiles, duplicates, and missing publications. After sending personalized reports to the researchers, many quickly fixed their profiles and linked them together, making their online records clearer and more complete.

**Impact** – Fixing these scattered profiles helped researchers get proper credit for their work. For example, one professor’s citation count more than doubled after merging two profiles. This audit showed that keeping digital identities accurate helps researchers be better recognized and supports fair evaluation of their work.

**What’s next step** – The university created easy-to-follow videos to help researchers maintain their profiles. Other institutions can use similar audits and tools to improve the accuracy of academic records worldwide, leading to better collaboration and trust in research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By auditing and correcting online profiles, the University of Bath helped researchers show their true work and achievements more clearly.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’université aide les chercheurs à regrouper leurs profils en ligne dispersés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux chercheurs ont plusieurs profils en ligne qui listent leurs travaux, mais ces profils peuvent être incomplets, dupliqués ou incorrects. Cela complique la visibilité et la vérification de leur parcours.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – À l’Université de Bath au Royaume-Uni, des bibliothécaires ont examiné plus de 1 000 profils de chercheurs sur trois plateformes : ORCID, Pure et Scopus. Ils ont découvert de nombreuses erreurs comme des profils vides, des doublons et des publications manquantes. Après avoir envoyé des rapports personnalisés, plusieurs chercheurs ont rapidement corrigé et relié leurs profils, rendant leurs dossiers en ligne plus clairs et complets.

**Impact** – Corriger ces profils dispersés a permis aux chercheurs d’obtenir un crédit juste pour leurs travaux. Par exemple, le nombre de citations d’un professeur a plus que doublé après la fusion de deux profils. Cet audit montre que maintenir des identités numériques précises aide à mieux reconnaître les chercheurs et à évaluer leurs travaux équitablement.

**Prochaine étape** – L’université a créé des vidéos simples pour aider les chercheurs à gérer leurs profils. D’autres institutions peuvent adopter des audits similaires pour améliorer la fiabilité des données académiques dans le monde, favorisant ainsi la collaboration et la confiance.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En auditant et en corrigeant les profils en ligne, l’Université de Bath a aidé les chercheurs à mieux refléter leur travail et leurs réussites.
articles.personality_title_es: La universidad ayuda a investigadores a unificar sus perfiles digitales dispersos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos investigadores tienen varios perfiles en línea que muestran su trabajo, pero estos perfiles pueden estar incompletos, duplicados o con errores. Esto dificulta que otros vean todo su trabajo o verifiquen su experiencia correctamente.

**Qué pasó** – En la Universidad de Bath en Reino Unido, bibliotecarios revisaron más de 1,000 perfiles de investigadores en tres plataformas: ORCID, Pure y Scopus. Encontraron muchos errores como perfiles vacíos, duplicados y publicaciones faltantes. Tras enviar informes personalizados, muchos investigadores corrigieron y unieron sus perfiles, haciendo sus registros en línea más claros y completos.

**Impacto** – Corregir estos perfiles dispersos ayudó a los investigadores a recibir el crédito justo por su trabajo. Por ejemplo, el conteo de citas de un profesor se más que duplicó al fusionar dos perfiles. Esta auditoría mostró que mantener identidades digitales precisas ayuda a reconocer mejor a los investigadores y a evaluar su trabajo de forma justa.

**Próximos pasos** – La universidad creó videos fáciles de seguir para ayudar a los investigadores a mantener sus perfiles. Otras instituciones pueden usar auditorías similares para mejorar la precisión de los registros académicos a nivel mundial, lo que favorece la colaboración y la confianza.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Al auditar y corregir perfiles en línea, la Universidad de Bath ayudó a los investigadores a mostrar con mayor claridad su trabajo y logros.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a scholar’s fragmented digital identity symbolized by multiple overlapping translucent profiles made of softly glowing data points and academic icons—such as open books, citation chains, and university crests—being gently gathered and connected by luminous threads held by a pair of caring librarian hands, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.
